obus 2026.01.30
  • Reference
  • Articles
    • DATRAS data access points
    • auxiliaries
    • Case examples
    • HL record types
    • Sanity testing

    HL record types

    hl-record-types.Rmd

    Overview

    The DATRAS length table (HL) is not a uniform table of length-frequency observations. A substantial minority of records carry no LengthClass at all, and even within the length-bearing records there are structural differences driven by the haul-level DataType field and the presence of sex or development-stage annotations. Treating all rows the same leads to silent errors in abundance or CPUE calculations.

    dr_add_record_type() classifies every row of a processed HL table into one of ten mutually exclusive integer types. The companion object hl_record_type_lookup is the lookup table that maps those integers to labels and descriptions.

    The classification function

    The function requires that dr_add_n_and_cpue() has already been called (so n_haul is present). It uses n_haul as a validity proxy: if n_haul is NA for a length-bearing record the haul is invalid (DataType of -9 or NA in the HH table).

    library(tidyverse)
library(obus)

# Typical pipeline: load → join HaulDuration → compute n_haul → classify
hh <- dr_hl(survey = "NS-IBTS")   # haul header
hl <- dr_hl(survey = "NS-IBTS")   # length table (illustrative; use actual loaders)

hl_typed <- hl |>
  left_join(hh |> select(.id, DataType, HaulDuration), by = ".id") |>
  dr_add_n_and_cpue() |>
  dr_add_record_type()

    For this article the full multi-survey dataset (HL joined with HH) was used, covering all surveys available in the DATRAS parquet store.

    Record type lookup 

    library(obus)
hl_record_type_lookup
#>    record_type lc_present                                       label
#> 1            1       TRUE                  Length-frequency, standard
#> 2            2       TRUE         Length-frequency, sex-disaggregated
#> 3            3       TRUE    Length-frequency, with development stage
#> 4            4       TRUE              Length-frequency, invalid haul
#> 5           10      FALSE                         Explicit zero catch
#> 6           11      FALSE                         Bulk bycatch weight
#> 7           12      FALSE               Counted catch, no subsampling
#> 8           13      FALSE            Subsampled catch summary, no sex
#> 9           14      FALSE Subsampled catch summary, sex-disaggregated
#> 10          15      FALSE                       Sex-coded null record
#> 11          16      FALSE Companion weight to length-frequency record
#> 12          99      FALSE                        Other / unclassified
#>                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              description
#> 1                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           LengthClass and n_haul present; no SpeciesSex or DevelopmentStage annotation
#> 2                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                LengthClass and n_haul present, SpeciesSex present, no DevelopmentStage
#> 3                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     LengthClass and n_haul present, DevelopmentStage present; ~99% also carry SpeciesSex. Seen mainly in cephalopod/maturity protocols (e.g. Sepia officinalis in BTS)
#> 4                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                LengthClass present but n_haul is NA: DataType = -9 or NA in the haul header (HH table)
#> 5                                                                                           All measurement vars absent (TotalNumber, SpeciesCategoryWeight, SubsamplingFactor, SpeciesSex all NA). SpeciesValidity = 5 ('not found') in ~94% of cases. These are standard-species-list absences recorded for hauls where the species was looked for but not caught; distinct from implicit zeros (hauls that simply omit the species). Concentrated in BTS and NS-IBTS.
#> 6                                                                                                                                                                                     LengthClass absent; SpeciesCategoryWeight present, TotalNumber absent; no type-3 record for the same .id + ValidAphiaID. Represents organisms weighed as bulk material without individual counting or length measurement: sponges, hydroids, algae, tunicates, etc. Primarily BTS.
#> 7                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         LengthClass absent; TotalNumber present, SubsamplingFactor absent. Species was counted (and possibly weighed) but no length measurements were taken and no subsampling structure was recorded.
#> 8                                                                                       LengthClass absent; SubsamplingFactor present, SpeciesSex absent. In ~99.8% of cases standalone: species was counted with a subsampling factor but not individually measured. In the rare remaining cases (~0.2%) acts as a companion header row that duplicates the totals from co-occurring type-1 length-frequency records for the same .id + ValidAphiaID + SpeciesCategory.
#> 9                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        LengthClass absent; SubsamplingFactor and SpeciesSex present. Analogous to type 13 but with sex annotation. Standalone in ~99.4% of cases; in ~0.6% acts as a companion header to co-occurring type-2 length-frequency records.
#> 10                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  LengthClass absent; SpeciesSex present but TotalNumber, SpeciesCategoryWeight, and SubsamplingFactor all absent. Functionally equivalent to type 10 (explicit zero) with a sex code attached. Only ~100 records in the full dataset.
#> 11 LengthClass absent; SpeciesCategoryWeight present, TotalNumber absent; a length-frequency record (type 1, 2, or 3) exists for the same .id + ValidAphiaID. The weight duplicates the SpeciesCategoryWeight already carried by the co-occurring length records and should be excluded to avoid double-counting. Seen for Sepia officinalis (BTS, alongside type-3 development-stage records) and Mnemiopsis leidyi (DYFS, alongside type-2 sex-disaggregated records).
#> 12                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   LengthClass absent; does not fit any of types 10-16

    Distribution across all surveys 

    hl_typed |>
  count(record_type, sort = TRUE) |>
  left_join(hl_record_type_lookup |> select(record_type, label),
            by = "record_type") |>
  mutate(pct = round(100 * n / sum(n), 2)) |>
  select(record_type, label, n, pct)
    record_type label n %
    1 Length-frequency, standard 7,450,315 52.9
    2 Length-frequency, sex-disaggregated 6,226,236 44.2
    3 Length-frequency, with development stage 24,705 0.17
    4 Length-frequency, invalid haul 24,298 0.17
    10 Zero/null catch 65,049 0.46
    11 Weight-only summary 21,097 0.15
    12 Count (+/- weight) summary, no subsampling 36,834 0.26
    13 Subsampled summary, no sex 97,716 0.69
    14 Subsampled summary, sex-disaggregated 146,374 1.04
    15 Sex-coded null record 100 <0.01

    The great majority of records (97.3 %) carry a LengthClass and a computable n_haul. Of those, roughly 46 % are additionally annotated by sex (type 2) and a small fraction by development/maturity stage (type 3). The remaining 2.7 % are summary or null records discussed in detail below.

    Records with LengthClass (types 1–4)

    These are the rows that drive length-frequency and abundance analyses. n_haul is calculated from NumberAtLength, SubsamplingFactor, and (for DataType == "C") HaulDuration:

    DataType n_haul formula
    C — CPUE NumberAtLength × SubsamplingFactor × HaulDuration / 60
    R — by haul NumberAtLength × SubsamplingFactor
    P — pseudocategory NumberAtLength × SubsamplingFactor
    S — subsampled NumberAtLength × SubsamplingFactor
    -9 or NA NA (invalid haul, type 4)

    Type 3 note: DevelopmentStage takes priority in the classification. A record with both DevelopmentStage and SpeciesSex present is assigned type 3 (~24 600 such records exist, mostly Sepia officinalis in BTS).

    Filtering for standard abundance analysis 

    hl_lf <- hl_typed |>
  filter(record_type %in% 1:3)   # drops invalid hauls (type 4)

    To restrict further to unsexed/aggregated data (e.g., when sexed and unsexed rows for the same length class would double-count):

    hl_lf_standard <- hl_typed |>
  filter(record_type == 1)

    Records without LengthClass (types 10–15)

    All records in this group have LengthClass, LengthCode, and NumberAtLength equal to NA, so n_haul is always NA. They should be excluded from length-frequency and abundance analyses.

    Type 10 — Zero/null catch

    These 65 049 records represent species that were explicitly recorded as absent from a haul. Two findings support this interpretation:

    1. SpeciesValidity is 5 (“species not found”) in 93.8 % of cases and 0 in the remaining 6.2 %.
    2. In 97 % of hauls that contain a type-10 record, at least one other species was measured and assigned a length-frequency record — confirming the haul was valid and the species was simply not caught.
    # Confirm: hauls with type-10 records mostly also have length data for other species
hauls_with_zero <- hl_typed |>
  filter(record_type == 10L) |>
  distinct(.id)

hl_typed |>
  semi_join(hauls_with_zero, by = ".id") |>
  filter(record_type %in% 1:3) |>
  summarise(hauls_also_with_length = n_distinct(.id))
# 7 080 out of 7 267 (97 %)

    Type-10 records are concentrated in BTS and NS-IBTS:

    Survey Records Hauls Species
    BTS 43,388 4,679 106
    NS-IBTS 20,486 1,690 80
    DYFS 784 652 22
    BITS 155 54 16
    SNS 228 184 6

    These are standard-species-list absences — exactly the explicit zero-catch entries needed when computing indices that require hauls with zero catch to be included. They should not be confused with hauls that simply don’t mention a species (implicit zeros).

    Type 11 — Weight-only summary

    These 21 097 records carry only SpeciesCategoryWeight; TotalNumber, SubsampledNumber, and SubsamplingFactor are all NA. Two distinct sub-populations exist.

    Sub-population A — Bulk bycatch (non-fish taxa): The dominant pattern (20 819 of 20 836 distinct haul × species combinations). Species are invertebrates, algae, and other organisms for which individual length measurement is not practical: sponges (Alcyonium, Porifera, Suberites), hydroids, nemerteans, algae (Fucus, Ulva), tunicates, etc. These records appear primarily in BTS (17 452 records) with smaller contributions from DYFS, NS-IBTS, BITS, PT-IBTS, SNS, and SE-SOUND. For these entries the SpeciesCategoryWeight is the total wet weight of the bulk material and is the only meaningful quantity.

    Sub-population B — Companion weight records (Sepia officinalis, BTS; Mnemiopsis leidyi, DYFS): Only 17 haul × species combinations mix type-11 with length-frequency records. Inspection of those hauls reveals a consistent four-record-type pattern per haul:

    # Typical structure for one Sepia haul
hl_typed |>
  filter(.id == "BTS:2021:3:BE:45CE:BT4A:36:60", ValidAphiaID == 141444L) |>
  distinct(record_type, SpeciesCategory, TotalNumber, SubsampledNumber,
           SubsamplingFactor, SpeciesCategoryWeight) |>
  arrange(record_type)
    record_type SpeciesCategory TotalNumber SubsampledNumber SubsamplingFactor SpeciesCategoryWeight
    1 1 2 2 1 538
    3 1 NA NA 1 28
    11 1 NA NA 1 28
    13 1 2 2 1 538

    The type-3 (development-stage length-frequency) and type-11 records carry identical SpeciesCategoryWeight values within each haul. The type-11 entry is a companion weight record that reports the total weight for the development-stage category — it is not a standalone bulk weight. The type-1 (standard length-frequency) and type-13 (subsampled summary) records carry the overall haul weight. For Sepia officinalis this pattern reflects the Belgian BTS protocol of recording both individual maturity-at-length data and a companion total weight for that maturity group.

    Practical consequence: For bulk bycatch (Sub-population A) the weight in type-11 records is the only information available and can be used in bycatch weight reporting. For Sepia in BTS (Sub-population B) the weight is already captured in the co-occurring type-3 records; the type-11 record is redundant and should be ignored to avoid double-counting.

    # Bycatch weight per haul for bulk taxa (excluding Sepia companion records)
bycatch_weights <- hl_typed |>
  filter(record_type == 11L) |>
  anti_join(
    hl_typed |>
      filter(record_type %in% 1:3) |>
      distinct(.id, ValidAphiaID),
    by = c(".id", "ValidAphiaID")
  ) |>
  group_by(.id, ValidAphiaID, latin) |>
  summarise(total_weight_g = sum(SpeciesCategoryWeight, na.rm = TRUE),
            .groups = "drop")

    Types 12–14 — Summary records without length

    These records summarise catch totals at the haul × species level but carry no length distribution. They appear when a species was caught but either not subsampled for length measurement (type 12) or subsampled with a factor but without individual length classes being recorded (types 13–14).

    Type Key variables present Typical use
    12 TotalNumber, optionally SpeciesCategoryWeight Whole-catch totals; no subsampling
    13 TotalNumber, SubsamplingFactor (± weight) Subsampled catch aggregate, no sex
    14 TotalNumber, SubsamplingFactor, SpeciesSex (± weight) Sex-disaggregated catch aggregate

    These records can supply total catch numbers and weights when length information is not needed, but they must not be used to derive length frequencies. Types 13 and 14 are the no-length counterparts of the sex-disaggregated type-10 records found in surveys such as NS-IBTS and BTS.

    Type 15 — Sex-coded null record

    One hundred records with SpeciesSex present but all other measurement fields absent. These are effectively sex-coded zero-catch entries. They can safely be treated as type-10 equivalents for most purposes.

    Summary: recommended filters by use case 

    # Length-frequency and abundance / CPUE analysis
hl_for_lf <- hl_typed |>
  filter(record_type %in% 1:3)

# Unsexed only (avoid double-counting in surveys with mixed sex/unsexed rows)
hl_unsexed <- hl_typed |>
  filter(record_type == 1)

# Explicit zero-catch entries (for index calculations needing true zeros)
hl_zeros <- hl_typed |>
  filter(record_type == 10L)

# Bulk bycatch weights (non-fish taxa, excluding Sepia companion records)
hl_bycatch_wt <- hl_typed |>
  filter(record_type == 11L) |>
  anti_join(
    hl_typed |> filter(record_type %in% 1:3) |> distinct(.id, ValidAphiaID),
    by = c(".id", "ValidAphiaID")
  )

    Records of types 10–15 all have n_haul = NA. Any pipeline that passes these rows to functions expecting valid abundance values will silently propagate NAs or, worse, drop rows that should be explicit zeros. Filter to types 1–3 before computing length frequencies or indices.

    Developed by Einar Hjorleifsson, Martin Pastoors, Vaishav Soni.

    Site built with pkgdown 2.2.0.
